coredata.dc:title: Preparation of graphene oxide-graphene quantum dots hybrid and its application in cancer theranostics
coredata.dc:creator: Kumawat, Mukesh Kumar
Thakur, Mukeshchand
Bahadur, Rohan
Kaku, Tanvi
R.S., Prabhuraj
Suchitta, Aakansha
Srivastava, Rohit
coredata.dc:description: Currently, an enormous amount of cancer research based on two-dimensional nano-graphene oxide (GO), as well as zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQDs), is being carried out in the fields of therapeutics and diagnostics. However, the exploration of their hybrid “functional” nanomaterials in the theranostic system is still rare. In the current study, a stable complex of GO and GQDs was formed by an electrostatic layer-by-layer assembly via a polyethylene imine bridge (GO-PEI-GQDs). Furthermore, we compared separate mono-equivalents of the GO-PEI-GQDs complex - GO and GQDs, in terms of cell imaging (diagnostics), photothermal, and oxidative stress response in breast cancer cells (MDA-MB-231). GO-PEI-GQDs showed an excellent photothermal response (44–49 °C) upon 808 nm laser (0.5 W cm−2) exposure for 5 min at a concentration up to 50 μg/mL. We report new synergistic properties of GO-PEI-GQDs such as stable fluorescence imaging and enhanced photothermal and cytotoxic activities on cancer cells. Composite materials made up of GO and GQDs combining diverse properties help to study 2D-0D heterosystems and improve specific therapeutic systems in theranostics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene oxide
Graphene quantum dots
Photothermal
Photodynamic therapy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photothermal response and cyclic photothermal stability
Biocompatibility, DNA profile analysis, and cellular uptake
Photothermal and photodynamic effect on MDA-MB-231 cells
Synthesis of GO, GQDs, and GO-PEI-GQDs
Photothermal response and cyclic photothermal stability studies
In vitro photothermal studies of cancer cells

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors acknowledge Sophisticated Analytical Instrumentation Facility (SAIF), Industrial Research Consultancy Centre (IRCC), Indian Institute of Technology Bombay (IITB), for providing central instrumentation facility. MKK acknowledges the Ministry of Human Resource Development (MHRD), India for financial assistance in a form of Teaching Assistantship (TA). Authors also acknowledge Dr. Radhika Poojari for fruitful comments and discussions on the manuscript.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of GQDs and formation of GO-PEI-GQDs hybrid. (a) schematic showing synthesis of GQDs through a hydrothermal‑carbonization process, (b) optical images showing GQDs (before and after dialysis) under daylight and UV lamp (375 nm), (c) stability of dispersed GO and GO: PEI solution at various ratios (w/w). (d) Synthesis of GO and its exfoliation. (e) Functionalization of GO sheets using PEI to form GO-PEI. The GQDs were then functionalized onto GO-PEI sheets to form GO-PEI-GQDs using layer-by-layer electrostatic assembly.

Optical characterization of bare GO, GQDs, and GO-PEI-GQDs (1:2) complex. (a) UV–Vis absorption spectra at a concentration of 100 μg/mL (inset showing Tyndall effect), (b) fluorescence spectrum excited at 300 nm, (c) excitation-dependent fluorescence emission of GO-PEI-GQDs, (d) showing variation in the surface charge during each step of formation of GO-PEI-GQDs.

XPS and Raman spectral analysis of GO, GQDs, and GO-PEI-GQDs. (a) Comparative XPS survey spectrum of surfaces of GO, GQDs, and GO-PEI-GQDs. (b) Deconvoluted XPS spectra showing further differentiation of the peaks into C 1 s, O 1 s, and N 1 s. (c) Comparative Raman spectral analysis.

TEM images of (a) GO sheets, (b) GO-PEI, inset showing SAED pattern, (c) GO-PEI-GQDs, (d-f) high-resolution images of the GO-PEI-GQDs hybrid where the GQDs are seen decorated over GO sheet. (g-i) Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) showed GQDs decorated GO sheet (GO-PEI-GQDs) exhibits green (h) and red fluorescence (i). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

In vitro photothermal response of GO, GQDs, and GO-PEI-GQDs. (a–b) Showing an increase in photothermal induced temperature with increasing the laser exposure time for bare GO and GO-PEI-GQDs. Furthermore, GO-PEI-GQDs shows comparable cyclic photothermal stability upon repetitive ON-OFF laser exposure (c) bare GQDs, (d–f) bare GO (10, 30, and 50 μg/mL), (g–i) GO-PEI-GQDs (10, 30, and 50 μg/mL).
Cell viability studies on L929 cells (a,c,e) and MDA-MB-231 cells (b,d,f) with different amounts of GO, GQDs, and GO-PEIGQDs (1:2 ratio).

Bioimaging of fibroblasts and cancer cells. Cell morphology and uptake of the GQDs and GO-PEI-GQDs (1:2) in normal murine fibroblast cells (L929) and breast cancer cells (MDA-MB-231). It is clearly visible that the GO-PEI-GQDs attach the surface of the cells with strong interaction.
The photothermal effect of GO, GQDs, GO-PEI-GQDs (1:2) on MDA-MB-231 cells.
ROS production analysis (a-d) without laser exposure and (e-h) with laser exposure in MDA-MB-231 cells (a, e) untreated cells (b & f) cells treated GO, (c, g) GQDs, and (d, h) GO-PEI-GQDs.

head.ce:title: Preparation of graphene oxide-graphene quantum dots hybrid and its application in cancer theranostics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biosciences and Bioengineering, Indian Institute of Technology Bombay (IIT Bombay), Mumbai, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Indian Institute of Technology Bombay (IIT Bombay)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biosciences and Bioengineering, Indian Institute of Technology Bombay (IIT Bombay), Mumbai, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Research in Nanotechnology & Science (CRNTS), IIT Bombay, Mumbai, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center for Research in Nanotechnology & Science (CRNTS)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center for Research in Nanotechnology & Science (CRNTS), IIT Bombay, Mumbai, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Biosciences and Bioengineering, Indian Institute of Technology Bombay, Mumbai, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Currently, an enormous amount of cancer research based on two-dimensional nano-graphene oxide (GO), as well as zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQDs), is being carried out in the fields of therapeutics and diagnostics. However, the exploration of their hybrid “functional” nanomaterials in the theranostic system is still rare. In the current study, a stable complex of GO and GQDs was formed by an electrostatic layer-by-layer assembly via a polyethylene imine bridge (GO-PEI-GQDs). Furthermore, we compared separate mono-equivalents of the GO-PEI-GQDs complex - GO and GQDs, in terms of cell imaging (diagnostics), photothermal, and oxidative stress response in breast cancer cells (MDA-MB-231). GO-PEI-GQDs showed an excellent photothermal response (44–49 °C) upon 808 nm laser (0.5 W cm−2) exposure for 5 min at a concentration up to 50 μg/mL. We report new synergistic properties of GO-PEI-GQDs such as stable fluorescence imaging and enhanced photothermal and cytotoxic activities on cancer cells. Composite materials made up of GO and GQDs combining diverse properties help to study 2D-0D heterosystems and improve specific therapeutic systems in theranostics.

ce:section.ce:para: Chemotherapy of cancers stays with several drawbacks, which has brought photothermal therapy (PTT) into the picture as a new strategy to fight them[1,2]. The PTT employs photothermal agents that are directly injected into the cancerous site or targeted using targeting agents. These photothermal agents are designed in such a way that they get excited preferably by the near-infrared (NIR) radiations and release heat during relaxation, that causes damage to the cancer cells. For an effective eradication of the cancer cells, theranostic systems i.e. nanosystems that deal with both diagnosis and therapy are proposed, and researchers have been exploring several aspects of such systems [3]. To date, several photothermal agents have been researched that include organic, inorganic, organo-inorganic nanomaterials and their composites [4–6]. Carbon nanomaterials, specifically carbon nanotubes, graphene, and graphene derivatives are popular for their extraordinary composition and excellent inherent properties for diverse applications such as fingerprinting, photocatalysis, electromagnetic shielding, and electric applications [7–15]. These carbon nanomaterials have pragmatically intervened in the field of biomedical engineering also and have been contributing to the research in tissue engineering, drug delivery, biosensing, bioimaging, and cancer theranostics [16,17]. An oxidized form of graphite, graphene oxide (GO), is a highly explored carbon nanomaterial since last decade [18–20]. Unlike graphene, graphene oxide has an advantage of its dispersibility in an aqueous medium, which encourages its exploration for biological applications. While, for graphene, it is important to chemically tailor its surface to make it water dispersible and compatible with extra/intracellular environment [21–23]. The GO sheets exhibit inherent toxicity as the process of their synthesis and tailoring their surface involves hazardous and toxic reagents and traces of which may remain with the material to exhibit toxicity further in vitro/in vivo [23–25].

Graphene-based oxides are inherently poorly fluorescent material. To realize their application in diagnostics, theranostics, and biomedical optics, one needs to conjugate these nanomaterials with an organic dye/imaging probe to locate them in the extra/intracellular environment [23,26,27]. The main concern of these dyes is their photo-instability, blinking and sensitivity to the environment which reduces their efficiency and undermines the purpose [28]. To address these issues, we use graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a zero-dimensional derivative of graphene oxide which bears excellent physicochemical properties like excellent fluorescence property, photostability, and biocompatibility to name a few [29,30]. The GQDs are easy to synthesize using various carbon-based precursors and thereby widely employed in bioimaging and photothermal response [29–32].

Taking an advantage of these properties of GQDs, we have functionalized the GO sheets with GQDs using polyethyleneimine (PEI) as a linker. The PEI is a cationic polymer that has been used in previous reports for surface modification of graphene nanomaterials, linking of two nano-constructs or moieties, chemical scissoring, biosensing, gene delivery and bioimaging, and fluorescence enhancing applications [33–37]. Since PEI is positively charged, it helps in bringing two negatively charged moieties together and make a stable hybrid. We performed a detailed optical and physicochemical characterization of this hybrid and their individual analogues. Furthermore, we demonstrated a comparative analysis of their properties such as cellular imaging, photothermal response to MDA-MB-231 cancer cells, cytotoxicity, and oxidative stress generation for cancer therapy. This work suggests a synergistic treatment of cancer cells with a better photothermal response even at laser power density as low as 0.5 W cm−2 which is lower than a report showed recently [38].

The detailed synthesis methods have been provided in the experimental section (Section 3). Fig. 1 shows a schematic for the preparation of the GO-PEI-GQDs hybrid. The GQDs are synthesized by a hydrothermal method using L-glutathione as a carbon precursor (Fig. 1a). The high-temperature ca. 150 °C causes carbonization of the L-glutathione and produces GQDs (Fig. 1a-b). The GO dispersion has excellent stability without any chunk of it settling at the bottom. However, after addition of PEI, negatively charged GO sheets get attracted towards positively charged PEI and bind by electrostatic force (Fig. 1e) [33,39]. Once the GO sheets are attached with PEI, the charge of the sheets gets inverted where the PEI binds and these sheets further attach to other negatively charged GO sheets present in the surrounding that causes agglomeration of the GO sheets in the solution after PEI addition. The weak force between the agglomerated sheets is avoided by bath sonication and probe sonication to segregate the sheets forming a stable well-dispersed GO-PEI solution (Fig. 1c). The attachment of the PEI polymeric layer (cationic) onto the GO sheets (anionic surface) causes net charge inversion of the resulting GO-PEI sheets (positively charged), as has been explained in Section 2.2. This enables stable attachment of the GQDs (anionic) through the layer-by-layer assembly.

The UV–visible absorption analysis as shown in Fig. 2a shows characteristic absorption peaks of GO and GQDs at 232 and 230 nm and shoulders around 300 and 275 nm corresponding to π-π* and n-π* electronic transitions, respectively. The GO-PEI-GQDs solution was very well-dispersed in aqueous solution and showed wide absorption in the range of 275–360 nm arising out of cumulative absorption of GO and GQDs.

The GO-PEI-GQDs solution shows Tyndall effect as seen in Fig. 2a inset. In Fig. 2b, it was observed that the bare GO did not show any photoluminescence property. The photoluminescence spectra of the GO-PEI-GQDs arise out of the inherent property of the attached GQDs. The GO is known to decrease the photo-emission of the fluorescent molecule via photo-induced charge transfer from the fluorophore to the oxide surface, thus, quenching the fluorescence of the fluorophore [33,40,41]. Consequently, we observed a decrement in the photoluminescence emission from the GO-PEI-GQDs in contrast to bare GQDs (Fig. 2b). We further investigated the effect of PEI and GO on the photoluminescence of the GQDs. The addition of different amounts of PEI in the GQDs solution show increment in the photoluminescence intensity of the GQDs which comes down sharply after subsequent addition of the GO to the solution (Fig. S1a). Hence, the PEI not only acts as a linker but also acts as a fluorescence enhancer that ultimately rescues the photoluminescence intensity of the GQDs from getting significantly quenched by GO [35]. However, it demonstrated that the net resultant fluorescence showed sufficient fluorescence emission for cellular studies. Further, the optical characterization of GO-PEI-GQDs shows their excitation-dependent photoluminescence emission (Fig. 2c and 4g-i) owing to their wide-absorption and subsequently giving multiple-emissions stretching from visible through the infrared spectrum. The photoluminescence of the GQDs varies with a change in pH of the solution (Fig. S1b-c). The change in the photoluminescence of the GQDs is attributed to the change in surface charge of the GQDs by protonation-deprotonation of the surface functional groups [29]. Surface charge analysis showed that initially, the aqueous dispersion of GO sheets and the GQDs possess overall negative charge −23.6 and − 3.01 mV, respectively. However, there was a change in the surface charge to +8 mV that confirmed the attachment of positively charged PEI via electrostatic attraction after removal of excess PEI (Fig. 2d and S2). The positive zeta-potential as +11.89, +13.33, and + 16.35 mV confirmed the formation of final GO-PEI-GQDs complex in different ratios 1:1, 1:2, 1:4, respectively (Fig. 2d). The formation of GO-PEI-GQDs hybrid was further confirmed by Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) (Fig. S2) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Fig. 3 a-b). A comparative XPS survey spectral analysis (Fig. 3a) reveals the presence of C, N, and O atoms on the surface of the GO-PEI-GQDs. The appearance of nitrogen peak in the survey spectrum of the hybrid is attributed to PEI and GQDs and confirms their attachment over the surface of the hybrid. Further, deconvoluted XPS spectra (C 1 s, O 1 s, and N 1 s) of GO, GQDs, and GO-PEI-GQDs have been shown in Fig. 3b. The C 1 s spectrum of GO shows the peaks of >C=C<, sp3 C(OH), –C-O-C-, and > C=O kinds of moieties at 284.4, 285.2, 286.0, and 287.5 eV, respectively (Fig. 3b) [42]. Both C 1 s and O 1 s spectra of GO show the abundance of oxygen-containing functional groups, but, N 1 s spectrum of GO appears flat that indicates the minuscule amount of nitrogen atoms as corroborated by its survey spectrum too. However, the N 1 s spectra of GQDs and GO-PEI-GQDs show intense peaks and indicate the presence of pyridinic and pyrrolic nitrogen atoms [43]. The deconvoluted spectra of GO-PEI-GQDs follow an almost similar pattern as of GQDs' that signifies the surface coating of GO sheets by the PEI and GQDs. Raman spectral analysis does not show any significant difference between different ratios of the hybrid (Fig. 3c) probably due to the overlapping of strong G and D-bands of GO.

Field emission gun Transmission electron microscopy (FEG-TEM) imaging of diluted GO dispersion showed that GO sheets (bare and PEI coated) are well-exfoliated Fig. 4 (a-b). Imaging of GO-PEI-GQDs showed that GQDs were decorated over GO sheets Fig. 4(c-d). High-resolution image of the hybrid showing GQDs of ~5–8 nm size nicely distributed Fig. 4(d). FEG-TEM imaging of laser treated GO-PEI-GQDs Fig. 4(e-f) showed that after the laser exposure (808 nm, 0.5 W cm−2, 15 min), the GQDs were attached over GO sheets. Furthermore, the CLSM imaging of the hybrid displayed fully illuminated multi-fluorescent GO sheet owing to the presence of GQDs over the sheet. We further aim to utilize by combining this multi-fluorescence property of the GO-PEI-GQDs to its photothermal property of GO [32,38] (Fig. 8).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal response and cyclic photothermal stability
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 shows in vitro photothermal response of GO, GQDs, and GO-PEI-GQDs using near-infrared (NIR) 808 nm laser (0.5 W cm−2) for 5 min, at an operating distance of 1 cm. The increase in temperature was recorded to observe the photothermal efficiency of the GO-PEI-GQDs in contrast to its mono-equivalents (GO and GQDs). The maximum temperature obtained in case of GO was ca. 45 °C (50 μg/mL) while for GO-PEI-GQDs the temperature rose up to 48 °C (Fig. 5 a, b) which is due to the conjugation of GQDs onto GO surface. It has been recently shown that GQDs can also act as photothermal agents [30] (Fig. 5c) and thereby improve the photothermal response of GO-PEI-GQDs. Further, the cyclic photothermal stability study showed that the photothermal response was quite stable up to five ON-OFF cycles of laser exposure. The photostability response indicates the robustness of the photothermal system and ensures its long-term use. Also, the hybrids show robustness in terms of the integrity of the attachment between GQDs and GO sheets under laser irradiation. The GQDs remain in abundance over the surface of the GO sheets even after laser exposure for 15 min. Furthermore, this can be useful for cancer cell tracking as the GQDs remain stable over the surface keeping the hybrid intact (Fig. S3) [30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility, DNA profile analysis, and cellular uptake
ce:section.ce:para: We performed in vitro cellular biocompatibility of bare GO, GQDs, and GO-PEI-GQDs (all ratios) (Fig. 6 , S4a-b). On L929 cells, it was observed that with increasing concentration of GO and GQDs, there was a decrease in cell viability (~15-20%) (Fig. 6a,c). The morphology of the L929 cells incubated with GO-PEI-GQDs (1:1 & 1:4) at 50–100 μg/mL was altered (Fig. S4a). However, the GO-PEI-GQDs did not show a significant toxic effect quantitatively on L929 cell growth (Fig. 6e, S4b). The morphology of the L929 cells treated with GO-PEI-GQDs (1:2) was found intact. The GO-PEI-GQDs caused toxicity in MDA-MB-231 cells at a concentration as high as 100 μg/mL. Thus, it was observed that the GO-PEI-GQDs (1:2), albeit lesser, showed selectivity on breast cancer cells in contrast to normal fibroblasts in terms of toxicity (Fig. 6f). We speculate that this could be probably due to increased interaction of GO-PEI-GQDs with over-expressed receptors on the cancer cell. The interaction and targeting potential of GO-PEI-GQDs on MDA-MB-231 cells requires further more understanding. The viability and proliferation of the cells in contact with nanomaterials is governed by several factors; the surface charge of the nanomaterial being one of them. For example, Tu et al. demonstrated that positively charged GO helps in neurite outgrowth and branching more than its neutral or negatively charged counterpart [44].

Furthermore, we performed cell cycle analysis to study the effect of bare GO, GQDs, and GO-PEI-GQDs on the phases of the cell cycle using flow cytometry. The cells treated with GO-PEI-GQDs (1:2) and GO did not affect the S-phase of the cell cycle, similar to the untreated control cells (Fig. S4c). The S-phase was decreased in the case of bare GQDs which shows that it slightly affects the normal synthesis of DNA during cell replication [30,45,46]. It has been observed in previous studies, when the GQDs were used at high concentrations (>100 μg/mL), they produced reactive oxygen species (ROS) and induced autophagy [30,46]. The GO and GO-PEI-GQDs (1:2) showed very less cell population (1.5 and 1.2%, respectively) in Sub-G1 phase, however, the GQDs and GO-PEI-GQDs (1:1 and 1:4) showed significant cell population (9.2, 16.2 & 16.6, respectively) in SubG1 phase. The increase in the SubG1 phase population is indicative of the apoptotic effect of the material [46]. To explore the cellular imaging potential and uptake of the hybrids, bare GQDs and GO-PEI-GQDs hybrids were incubated with L929 normal mouse fibroblasts and MDA-MB-231cancer cells, and further imaged under CLSM. The GO-PEI-GQDs (1:2) showed excellent uptake in both normal and cancer cells after 12 h of incubation (Fig. 7 ). A comparative cellular uptake of all ratios of the hybrid was also checked in MDA-MB-231 cancer cells (Figs. S5–S6). There are some important factors such as surface charge, shape, and size of the nanomaterials that govern the cellular uptake mechanism [48-50]. It has been previously demonstrated that positively charged nanomaterials get internalized more as compared to their neutral or negatively charged counterparts and this is attributed to the attraction between the negatively charged cell membrane and positively charged nanomaterials [48,50]. The endocytic pathway of cellular uptake of foreign substance is majorly determined by its size and it is categorized into phagocytosis (>1000 nm sized materials) and pinocytosis (nanoparticles of <1000 nm or liquid soluble substance) [50]. The pinocytosis is further divided into four subcategories: clathrin-mediated endocytosis (~120 nm), caveolae-mediated endocytosis (~60 nm), macropinocytosis, and clathrin/caveolae-independent endocytosis (~90 nm). However, the studies that were carried out within the last few years suggest that, the uptake of GO sheets occurs via both phagocytosis and pinocytosis [29,49–51]. Huang et al. studied cellular internalization of gold nanoparticles-decorated GO sheets into Ca Ski cells using surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) spectroscopy. They suggested that internalization of the GO sheets is an energy-dependent event that occurs via clathrin-mediated endocytosis [51]. Mu et al. demonstrated that internalization of protein-coated GO sheets is size dependent; small sized nanosheets favor clathrin-mediated internalization pathway whereas the increase in the size of the sheets increases the possibility of phagocytotic uptake [49].

Recently, Tu et al. revealed that the charge and size of the GO sheets, both play an important role in the internalization process. The internalization of negatively charged GO sheets depends strongly on their size, whereas positively charged sheets get internalized via either of the pathways, regardless of their size. In our report, since the size of the GO-PEI-GQDs is in the range of ~500–900 nm (Table S1) and the hybrid possesses a fairly cationic surface charge, we believe that it got internalized through both phagocytosis and clathrin-mediated endocytosis pathways as shown in a recent report [50]. Initially, the positively charged hybrids attach to negatively charged membrane of the cells and then gradually get internalized. It has already been shown in our previous report that the GQDs enter the cell primarily via clathrin and caveolae-mediated endocytosis. The internalization of the GO-PEI-GQDs into cells is marked by the fluorescence of the GQDs (Fig. 7 and Fig. S5–S6)) [29]. As expected, in conjugation with GO, the fluorescence of the GQDs is partially quenched which further showed the efficiency of conjugation. The GO-PEI-GQDs (1:1 and 1:2) show better uptake than GO-PEI-GQDs (1:4) (Fig. S5–S6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal and photodynamic effect on MDA-MB-231 cells
ce:section.ce:para: Having established the potential photothermal response and in vitro stability of the GO-PEI-GQDs, we treated the MDA-MB-231 cells with GO-PEI-GQDs (all hybrids) and performed a photothermal study. Fig. 8 shows cell viability after laser treatment of MDA-MB-231 cells incubated with bare GO, GQDs, and GO-PEI-GQDs (1:2). There was a significant decrease in the number of cancer cells before and after laser treatment in GO-PEI-GQDs (Fig. 8c) in comparison to bare GO and GQDs (Fig. 8 a-b). Untreated or only laser treated cells did not show any significant cytotoxicity, whereas after laser treatment there is a significant decrease in cell number (P < 0.001).

We also carried out a comparative photothermal ablation study of all hybrids. All hybrids significantly affect the cancer cells, but GO-PEI-GQDs (1:2) is the most effective (Fig. S7). The photothermal ablation of cells have been observed previously above 42 °C causing apoptosis due to hyperthermia [52,53]. Although carbon-based nanomaterials, especially GO, have been shown to induce photothermal response [33], the hybrid nature of GO-PEI-GQDs not only enhances their photothermal response but, also induce localized oxidative stress (or photodynamic effect). In order to further understand the cellular toxicity induced by GO-PEI-GQDs, we analyzed reactive oxygen species (ROS) production in cells before and after laser treatments. Fig. 9 shows flow cytometry-based ROS analysis of cancer cells treated with GO, GQDs, and GO-PEI-GQDs with and without laser treatment. It is observed that the cells incubated with GO, GQDs, and GO-PEI-GQDs do not produce any ROS when they are not treated with laser (Fig. 9a-d). However, when the cells are treated with laser after incubation with GO, GQDs, and GO-PEI-GQDs, they showed increased production of ROS inside the cells (Fig. 9e-h). It is shown that GQDs can cause ROS production [30,46], and in this case, both GO and GQDs showed a slight increase in the ROS (~ 24% and ~13%, respectively). Thus, their synergistic oxidative stress was observed in GO-PEI-GQDs (~66%) after laser treatment, which complements with the thermal stress. Thus, the cell viability of the MDA-MB-231 cells significantly decreases (~ 80% cell death) due to the synergistic activity of photothermal and photodynamic response. Therefore this complex could be used for photothermal complemented with photodynamic therapy.

Our work shows that the synthesis and application of fluorescent graphene-based nanohybrid “GO-PEI-GQDs” acts as a potential nano-theranostic system. The hybrid is beneficiary of excellent inherent properties of GQDs and GO which showed excellent biocompatibility and bioimaging in L929 and MDA-MB-231 cells. The hybrid is very effective even at its very low dose (50 μg/mL) and low power density (0.5 W cm−2) of the laser (808 nm). The cumulative photothermal and photodynamic properties of the hybrid delivers a synergistic treatment to cancer cells.

Graphite (powder, <20 μm, synthetic) and Poly-(ethyleneimine) solution (PEI, 50% w/v) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Orthophosphoric acid (H3PO4) and Potassium permanganate (KMnO4) were bought from Loba-Chemie, Sulphuric acid (H2SO4, 98%), Hydrochloric acid (HCl, 35%), and Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were procured from Merck Life Science Private Limited, Mumbai, India. Glutathione (GSH) was procured from Sisco Research Laboratories (SRL) Private Limited, India, Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30% w/w) was procured from Fisher Scientific company, Mumbai, India. Milli-Q water used as a solvent for the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GO, GQDs, and GO-PEI-GQDs

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene oxide synthesis was carried out by following the improved Hummers' method [54]. Briefly, 200 mg of graphite was mixed with an acid mixture of H2SO4/H3PO4 in 9:1 ratio (24:2.6 mL) and then 1.2 g of KMnO4 was added into it. The reaction mixture was then stirred at 50 °C for 12 h. The reaction was cooled down to room temperature followed by pouring it over ice water containing 30% H2O2 (200 μL). The reaction mixture was then washed by repetitive centrifugation cycles done at 4000 rpm for 4 h. The supernatant was discarded and the pellet was then washed with Milli-Q water, 35% HCl and ethanol. After several washing steps, the material was dispersed in 15.0 mL ether and filtered over PTFE membrane with a pore size of 0.45 μm. After vacuum drying at room temperature, 316 mg solid was obtained. GO dispersion was prepared as per the required concentration, which required bath sonication for 1.5 h to exfoliate the sheets and further probe sonication (400 W) for 20 min to cut-down the dimensions of the GO sheets.

The GQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method. Briefly, 0.4 g GSH was mixed with 20 mL of Milli-Q water (pH 6.5) at ambient temperature (29 ± 2 °C) using magnetic stirrer for 5 min. The solution was transferred to a Teflon coated autoclave and heated in a furnace at 150 °C for 8 h. The resultant solution was mixed with NaOH (5 mL, 0.1 M) and purified using dialysis. For purification, 1 mL of solution was dialyzed against 20 mL of Milli-Q water (pH 6.5) under stirring conditions at an ambient temperature (29 ± 2 °C) for 24 h. The dialyzed solution was light brown in colour and showed bright blue fluorescence under UV-lamp (375 nm). The solution was then lyophilized for 24 h to obtain solid-state GQDs. The aliquots of GQDs were prepared and stored.

Graphene oxide was exfoliated using bath sonication and probe sonication. For the preparation of GO-PEI-GQDs, briefly, PEI (5 mg/mL) was added into three different sample vials containing 2.0 mL of GO each (1.0 mg/mL) in different GO:PEI ratios such as 1:1, 1:2 and 1:4 for functionalization. While adding PEI into GO dispersions, brisk stirring is required as agglomeration of the GO sheets occur that can further be fixed by bath sonication treatment to the solutions. The mixture was stirred overnight at the ambient temperature. Residual PEI was washed thrice with Milli-Q at 20,000 rpm for 15 min. The pellets were dispersed well during the washing steps using a bath and a probe sonicator. Further, bare GQDs (1.0 mg/mL, 1:1 ratio maintained with bare GO) were added to GO:PEI conjugate and vigorously stirred for 24 h at the ambient temperature, followed by washing at 8000 rpm for 15 min to remove the unattached GQDs and get the final hybrid GO-PEI-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal response and cyclic photothermal stability studies
ce:section.ce:para: To study the photothermal response and cyclic photothermal stability study and 200 μL of each GO, GQDs and GO-PEI-GQDs (1:2) was added to different wells of 96 well plate which was already at 37 °C in a water bath. The temperature of the test samples was maintained at 37 °C before the experiment was carried out. Each sample well was shone by 808 nm laser (0.5 W cm−2) for 30 s and temperature was measured for 5 min. For cyclic photothermal stability study, same experiment (laser ON) was repeated for 5 times with the interval of 5 min (laser OFF). In each interval (laser OFF) of 5 min, the temperature was measured at 30 s each.

Murine fibroblast (L929) cells and breast cancer (MDA-MB-23) cells were procured from National Centre for Cell Sciences (NCCS), Pune. Cells were cultured and maintained in Minimal Essential Medium (MEM) supplemented with 10% Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS), antibiotic solution (100 units/mL of penicillin and 100 μg/mL of streptomycin). On reaching ca. 85% confluency, the cells were passaged using 1× Trypsin-EDTA solution. For cellular cytotoxicity, an MTT-based assay was performed in a 96-well plate with 5000 cells/well at 37 °C and 5% CO2. Followed by incubation for 24 h, the cells were treated with different concentrations of GO, GQDs, and GO-PEI-GQDs (all hybrids). After 24 h of incubation, cellular morphology was checked under an optical inverted microscope. The culture media from each well was removed and cells of each well were washed with 200 μL of PBS to ensure the complete removal of residual material. Freshly prepared MTT dye (5 mg/mL) was added to each well and the plate was kept for another 5 h of incubation. After careful removal of the culture media without interrupting the formazan crystals, 200 μL of DMSO was added in each well to dissolve the formazan crystals. The absorbance was recorded at 570 nm with the reference wavelength set at 620 nm. Percentage cell viability was then estimated based as shown in previous reports [45,55,56].

Multi-photon cellular bioimaging was performed on L929 and MDA-MB-231 cells cultured on sterile coverslips. Briefly, 20,000 cells of L929 and MDA-MB-231 were seeded in the wells of 12 well plates, each well was equipped with a sterile glass-coverslip. After 24 h of incubation under standard incubation conditions, the cells were treated with GQDs and GO-PEI-GQDs (all hybrids) and incubated for 12 h. Cell fixation was done using formaldehyde solution (3.7%) for 15 min followed by washing with PBS thrice. Cells were imaged under CLSM in green and red filters and images were captured in Z-stacks. The images were further processed using ZEN (blue edition) package to analyze the localization of the GO sheets across dimensions of the cell.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro photothermal studies of cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: For cellular photothermal studies, MDA-MB-231 cells were seeded in a 96-well plate at a density of 2500 cells/well. Cells were incubated at 37 °C and 5% CO2 for 24 h so as to ensure proper cell attachment to the substrate. The cells were treated with GO, GQDs, and GO-PEI-GQDs at different concentrations. A 808 nm laser was shone from a height of about 2–3 mm from the cell medium surface for 5 min. The cells were maintained at 37 °C by mounting the plate over a thermomixer instrument in a sterile environment throughout the experiment.

Flow cytometry was performed to understand the effect of nanomaterials on the cell cycle and ROS analysis. For cell cycle study, L929 cells were grown for 24 h, then trypsinized, washed thrice and resuspended in PBS (7.0). Chilled ethanol (70%) was then used to permeabilize the cells followed by washing thrice with PBS and then incubated with propidium iodide (50 μg/mL) and RNase A for 60 min at an ambient temperature. Subsequently, the cells were washed and resuspended in PBS. ROS experiment was carried out according to our previous report [30]. For FACS analysis, nearly 500 μL of cells were passed through a sterile filter into Flow cytometry ampoules and then analyzed for at least 10,000 events. The data was analyzed with FlowJo Version 10 developmental pack.

The morphology, size and high-resolution structural analysis of GO and GO-PEI-GQDs were carried out using Field Emission Gun-Transmission Electron Microscope (FEG-TEM) JEOL-2100F, Japan. UV–visible absorption spectra were recorded over a UV–Vis Spectrophotometer (Lambda25, Perkin Elmer). The Infrared (IR) spectra were obtained from Bruker, Germany and Raman spectra were recorded on Jobin-Yvon Labram spectrometer. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to record spectra using a monochromatic Al K-α source (225 W) with a Kratos Analytical, UK (Model AXIS Supra) instrument, for elemental analysis of surface of the nanomaterials. The size of the hybrid particles was measured using a Malvern Zetasizer instrument Ver. 7.11 (Malvern Instruments Ltd.). The cellular morphology was examined under inverted optical Nikon Eclipse Ts2 microscope at 10× resolution. For cell viability assay, the absorption was read by a Tecan Infinite Pro M 200 plate reader The cellular imaging and uptake was studied using Olympus FV500 and Zeiss Axio-Observer Z1 Confocal Laser Scanning Microscope (CLSM). Flowcytometry studies (Cell cycle and ROS) were carried out using a BD FACS Aria flow cytometer instrument.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors acknowledge Sophisticated Analytical Instrumentation Facility (SAIF), Industrial Research Consultancy Centre (IRCC), Indian Institute of Technology Bombay (IITB), for providing central instrumentation facility. MKK acknowledges the Ministry of Human Resource Development (MHRD), India for financial assistance in a form of Teaching Assistantship (TA). Authors also acknowledge Dr. Radhika Poojari for fruitful comments and discussions on the manuscript.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2019.109774.
